phase_metadata.title:
Java Basics: Variables, Data
Types, and Operators
phase_metadata.description:
Mastering the building blocks of Java
programming including data storage, type conversion, mathematical operations,
and user interaction.

learning_objectives: Understand how Java manages memory using primitive and reference data types

Identify and implement the three types of Java variables: local, instance, and
class
Perform safe type conversions using widening and narrowing casting
Apply arithmetic, logical, and relational operators to build program logic
Utilize the Scanner class to create interactive console applications
prerequisites:
Phase 1: Understanding of JDK installation and the basic
structure of a HelloWorld class.
introduction:
In the previous phase, we
learned how to run a basic Java program. Now, we dive into how Java actually
handles information. Imagine a program as a kitchen: the data types are the
ingredients, variables are the containers we store them in, and operators are
the tools (like knives or mixers) we use to process them. To make our programs
interactive, we will also learn how to listen to the user's input using the
Scanner tool.

concept_explanation.data_types:
Java is a
'statically-typed' language, meaning every variable must have a declared type.
Primitives (like int, double, boolean) are simple values stored directly in the
Stack memory. Reference types (like String or Scanner) are complex objects
stored in the Heap memory, with a pointer (address) kept in the Stack.
concept_explanation.variables:
Variables are named memory locations. Java provides three scopes:
Local (inside methods), Instance (inside classes, unique to every object), and
Class/Static (shared across all objects of a class).
concept_explanation.type_casting:
Casting
is converting one data type to another. Widening (e.g., int to double) happens
automatically because there is no risk of data loss. Narrowing (e.g., double to
int) requires manual syntax because it might truncate decimal values.
concept_explanation.operators:
Java uses symbols to perform operations. These include Arithmetic
(+, -, *, /, %), Relational (==, !=, >, <), Logical (&&, ||, !), and the Ternary
operator (condition ? true : false).
concept_explanation.scanner:
The Scanner class is part of
the java.util package. It reads input from the keyboard (System.in) and converts
it into usable Java types like nextInt() or nextLine().

syntax_breakdown.dataType:
The kind of data to
be stored (e.g., int for integers).
syntax_breakdown.variableName:
The identifier used to
refer to the value (must follow camelCase).
syntax_breakdown.assignment_operator:
The '='
symbol assigns the value on the right to the variable on the left.
syntax_breakdown.new_keyword: Used to create an instance of a reference type like Scanner.
syntax_breakdown.system_in: The standard input stream representing the keyboard.

rules_and_key_points:
Variable names cannot start with numbers and cannot
be Java keywords (like 'class' or 'void').

Local variables must be
initialized before they are used, or the code will not compile.

The 'final'
keyword can be added to a variable to make it a constant (unchangeable).

Integer division (e.g., 5 / 2) results in 2, not 2.5, unless one operand is a
double.

The '+' operator performs addition with numbers but concatenation
with Strings.
Boolean variables can only store 'true' or 'false'.

Floating-point literals are treated as double by default; use 'f' suffix for
floats (e.g., 3.14f).
rules_and_key_points.title: Primitive Types and Variables
rules_and_key_points.description:
This example
demonstrates how to declare different primitive types and perform basic math.

code_example_2.title: User Interaction with Scanner
code_example_2.description:
A
practical program that asks for user input and performs a narrowing type cast.

real_world_use_cases:
E-commerce: Using 'double' for prices and 'int' for
stock counts.

Authentication: Using 'boolean' to track if a user is logged
in.

Data Entry: Using 'Scanner' to capture user registration details in a
console-based CRM.

Financial Apps: Using explicit casting when converting
high-precision currency data into integers for simple reporting.
common_mistakes:
Attempting to store a large type (double) into a small
type (int) without an explicit cast.

Using 'next()' instead of 'nextLine()'
when the user input contains spaces.

Forgetting the 'f' suffix for float
literals, causing a 'possible loss of precision' error because Java defaults to
double.

Variable Shadowing: Declaring a local variable with the same name as
an instance variable, leading to confusion.

Integer Overflow: Adding to a
variable that has already reached its maximum value (e.g., Integer.MAX_VALUE
+ 1).
best_practices:
Always use meaningful variable names (e.g.,
'interestRate' instead of 'ir').

Use the 'final' keyword for values that
shouldn't change (constants).

Prefer 'double' over 'float' for better
precision in calculations.

Initialize variables at the point of declaration
whenever possible.

Always close the Scanner resource using a
try-with-resources block or manual close() call.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
What is the difference between
float and double?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Float is a 32-bit single-precision type (approx 7
decimal digits), while Double is a 64-bit double-precision type (approx 15
decimal digits). Use double for most calculations.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
Why do we
need the Scanner class?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Standard Java doesn't have a built-in
keyword to read keyboard input; Scanner is a utility class provided by the API
to bridge that gap.

summary:
Phase 2 covered the essential syntax for
storing data using various types and variables. We explored how to convert
types, perform operations, and interact with users via the console.
Understanding these basics is critical before moving to logic control.
key_takeaways: Primitives store values; Reference types store addresses.
Local variables live on the Stack; Objects live on the Heap.

Implicit
casting is safe; Explicit casting requires (type) syntax.

The '%' operator
returns the remainder of a division.

Scanner.nextLine() reads a whole line
including spaces.
Static variables belong to the class, not an object.

The JVM initializes instance
variables to default values (0, null, false), but not local variables.
preview_of_next_topic:
In Phase 3: Control Flow, we will learn how to make
decisions using if/else statements and repeat actions using loops.

questions_and_answers.question:
Explain what a
variable is in Java.
questions_and_answers.answer:
A variable is a container that holds data
while a Java program is executing. It represents a named memory location where
values can be stored and updated. Every variable must have a specific data type,
which determines what kind of data it can hold and how much memory it occupies.

questions_and_answers.question:
What is the difference between
Widening and Narrowing Casting?
questions_and_answers.answer:
Widening Casting (Automatic)
happens when you pass a smaller type to a larger type (e.g., int to double). It
is safe because there is no chance of losing data. Narrowing Casting (Manual)
happens when you pass a larger type to a smaller type (e.g., double to int).
This requires an explicit cast like '(int)' because decimal data or precision
might be lost.

The code fails
because 10.5 is a double and cannot be directly stored in an int. To fix it, use
an explicit cast: 'int x = (int) 10.5;'. This will store the value 10 in x.

questions_and_answers.question:
Write a code snippet that uses the
Ternary operator to check if a number is even or odd.
questions_and_answers.answer:
After calling nextInt(), a 'newline' character remains in the buffer.
nextLine() will consume that newline immediately and return an empty string for
the name. To fix this, add an extra sc.nextLine() after sc.nextInt() to clear
the buffer.
